text.title: Story Map Structure and Slicing Rules
text.summary: Two-axis map structure, backbone/task/story definitions, walking-skeleton contract, and vertical vs horizontal slicing rules.

rule.statement: Horizontal axis: backbone activities in narrative verb-phrases in the user's voice (e.g., "Find a place to stay", "Pay for the order"), ordered by user journey. Vertical axis: priority — walking skeleton stories at the top, enhancements below, nice-to-haves at the bottom.
rule.rationale: Activities expressed as feature names ("Search", "Checkout") produce a 2D Jira view, not a narrative. Verb-phrases force user-voice framing and reveal journey gaps that noun-names hide.

rule.statement: Freeze backbone activities before adding tasks. Backbone changes invalidate everything below. Require explicit unfreeze flag to reorder activities within a planning cycle.
rule.rationale: Activity churn mid-cycle forces task re-grouping and story re-assignment, wasting the most expensive agent work (the release-slicer pass).

rule.statement: Build one map per persona. If two personas share a journey, build two maps and derive a "shared backbone" view as a read-only overlay. Never start with a merged map.
rule.rationale: Multi-persona merged maps produce one-size-fits-none stories. Persona-specific maps surface persona-specific gaps; the shared backbone derives from the union after both are stable.

rule.statement: R1 (walking skeleton) must contain at least one story per backbone activity that delivers a complete, degraded-but-functional user journey end-to-end. Absence of any backbone activity in R1 is a contract violation, not a scope cut.
rule.rationale: A release that covers 4 of 5 activities completely but skips one does not let users complete the journey. An incomplete journey produces no learning about the product's core value proposition.

rule.statement: R1 capacity feasibility must be computed: sum of story points in R1 divided by (team velocity × sprint count) must be at most 1.0. Release-slicer must emit this ratio and refuse to mark R1 valid if it exceeds 1.0.
rule.rationale: Capacity overrun in R1 is the most common cause of walking-skeleton failure. Explicit feasibility evidence prevents optimistic slicing.

rule.statement: Tag enabler stories (auth, payment infra, telemetry, analytics) explicitly with enabler: true and pin them as dependencies of journey stories, not as journey tasks. Enablers must not count toward backbone coverage.
rule.rationale: Technical enablers are a prerequisite for journey stories but are not user-observable activities. Counting them as coverage masks an incomplete journey.

section.title: Slicing Strategy

rule.statement: Prefer vertical slicing (thin end-to-end slice covering all activities) over horizontal slicing (all tasks in one activity). Horizontal-first produces feature-factory releases with no complete journey.
rule.rationale: Vertical slicing maximizes learning per release by enabling end-to-end user tests from day one. Horizontal slicing defers learning until all activities are complete — often much later.

rule.statement: Map-as-code: the canonical artifact is map.yaml in version control. Miro, Markdown tables, and FigJam exports are rendered from map.yaml; they are display surfaces, not sources of truth.
rule.rationale: Visual tools are not queryable, diffable, or validatable. Tool fragmentation produces stale maps within days of a workshop. Code-as-source enables automated drift detection and Jira sync.

section.reference: YAML schema for story map with backbone, tasks, stories, releases, and capacity fields.
